data.title: CEF Insights: Midyear CEF Market Review And Outlook
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund and interval fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Jeff Margolin, Senior Vice President and Closed-End Fund Analyst with First Trust Portfolios. First Trust features a family of five closed-end funds, as well as four interval funds. On a quarterly basis, Jeff writes the Closed-End Fund review, which is available on ftportfolios.com.

We are happy to have you with us today, Jeff.

Jeff Margolin:

Thank you very much. I'm very happy to be able to speak with you today.

CEFA:

Jeff, we are just past the midpoint of 2025. Can you discuss the closed-end fund market over the first six months of the year?

Jeff Margolin:

Absolutely. It's been a very solid first six months of the year in terms of performance for many closed-end funds. Now, I think it's important to note that we are following two positive years from a performance standpoint for the average closed-end fund. Indeed, the average fund was up 10.6% in 2023 and then up another 15.3% in 2024. So the past two and a half years have been a very good time for the secondary market of closed-end funds from a performance standpoint. Now, specifically looking at the year to date data through June 30th, the average fund was up a solid 6.1%. Equity funds are up 11.8%. Now, I know that number does sound high relative to say the S&P 500, but it's important to remember that many equity closed-end funds have international exposure and several international equity markets have outperformed the S&P 500 so far year to date.

When we look at taxable fixed income closed-end funds, they're up through June 30th, 5.3%, and municipal closed-end funds are the only broad category that's lower and they are slightly lower by 0.26%. So other than municipal closed-end funds, all of the broad and all of the narrow categories of the closed-end fund marketplace that I track are positive so far year to date through June 30th. And from my perspective, I think the slight weakness that we've seen so far year to date in municipal closed-end funds represents an opportunity for investors. And perhaps we can talk about that more in a bit.

CEFA:

How do you see closed-end fund valuations in the current market?

Jeff Margolin:

In terms of valuations, the trend over the past several quarters has been for average discounts to NAV to narrow, and we ended the second quarter of the year with average discounts to NAV at -4.2%, which was narrower than the -4.5% level that they ended on March 31st. And when I look at average discounts to NAV for the closed-end fund marketplace or average discounts to NAV for a specific category or for an individual fund, I always look at the data relative to the long-term average, say 10 years, to help put the current data in perspective with historical trends. So, with an average discount to NAV of -4.2% as of June 30th, discounts are now narrower than the ten-year average discount to NAV of -5.8%. Now, keep in mind it was only 18 months ago on December 29th, 2023 when average discounts to NAV were nearly -10%, -9.6% to be exact.

And I believe it's encouraging to see average discounts to NAV continue to narrow. Periodically with valuations in the secondary market, average discounts to NAV will widen to high single, low double-digit levels as we saw in 2023, and it can create some short-term frustration for investors, but often it represents an opportunity as eventually investors recognize the value in the closed-end fund marketplace. They recognize that many funds are trading at discounts which are wider than their long-term average. And that is when what I think good old-fashioned price discovery takes hold and investors begin to buy closed-end funds at compelling valuations. And we slowly see average discounts begin to narrow. And I believe we have seen this process play out over the past several quarters. It's also helped that we've had a strong global equity market and solid performance of several key fixed income indices over the past several quarters.

When we look at discounts for equity funds, average discounts to NAV for equity funds also narrowed during the second quarter to -5.6% from the -6.2% level that they ended on March 31st. Average discounts to NAV for equity funds are narrower than a ten-year average discount of -7.2%. Looking at taxable bond, closed-end funds average discounts to NAV for taxable bond closed-end funds widened slightly during the second quarter to minus 0.8% from the minus 0.4% level that they ended on March 31st, but remain narrower than their ten-year average discount of -4.0%.

Among the broad categories, municipal closed-end funds remain the only category where average discounts to NAV are wider than their historical average. Average discounts to NAV for municipal closed-end funds narrowed during the second quarter to -6% from the -6.4% level that they ended on March 31st, but again remain wider than their ten-year average discount to NAV of -5.3%. It remains my view that if the Federal Reserve does indeed lower short-term interest rates at some point later this year, I expect average discounts for municipal closed-end funds to narrow further and potentially trade at discounts which are narrower than the long-term average.

CEFA:

Jeff, what are the key characteristics or metrics that investors should consider when evaluating a closed-end fund?

Jeff Margolin:

Well, right now in the secondary market, there are approximately 400 closed-end funds available in the secondary market right now for investors. So there's clearly plenty of options in all different categories for investors to consider. And I believe there are several key factors an investor should consider when investing in a closed-end fund. First, I think an investor needs to like the fundamentals and valuations of the underlying asset class that the fund is invested in because over time, historically, the share price of a closed-end fund will be highly correlated to the fund’s net asset value. And at the end of the day, a closed-end fund is an investment structure. It is not an asset class, but rather a closed-end fund is a way to gain exposure to various asset classes. And therefore, I think it's imperative that an investor favors the asset class that the closed-end fund invests in.

Now after an investor has made the decision that they like the specific asset class that the fund invests in, whether it be Munis, preferred, equities, et cetera. I think it's important to look at the valuation of the closed-end fund, the current premium or discount to NAV that a fund trades at. But importantly, it's crucial from my standpoint to look at the fund's current valuation relative to the historical average of the specific fund to gauge whether the fund is more expensive or cheaper relative to its historical average. I also think it's crucial to look at a fund's distribution and the composition of the distribution. We are seeing a trend of more funds distributing a return of capital in their distributions, and therefore I think it's important to see, and for an investor to see the breakdown of a closed-end fund’s distribution, how much is earned net investment income, how much is composed of realized capital gains, how much is composed of return of capital?

So, the composition of a fund is an important factor, as is the historical stability of a fund's distribution, at least from my perspective. I think it's also relevant to look at a fund's share price and NAV performance relative to their peers. When we're talking about closed-end funds, we can't ignore the fact that roughly two-thirds of all closed-end funds employ the use of leverage, and therefore, I believe it's important to look at a fund's leverage structure and the percent the fund is levered. If you're looking at investing in a fixed-income closed-end fund, I think an investor should look at the fund's credit quality breakdown, the duration and average maturity of the bonds in the fund. So, I could go on, but I think the six or seven factors that I just mentioned are a good place to start from my perspective.

CEFA:

What is your outlook for the rest of 2025?

Jeff Margolin:

Well, as the second half of the year commences, I think the biggest factor for the closed-end fund marketplace, particularly for fixed income closed-end funds, will be if the Fed cuts short-term rates. And if so, by how much? As discussed before, roughly two thirds of all closed-end funds employ the use of leverage. And if the Fed cuts rates say hypothetically two times during the back half of 2025, it will be very helpful for levered closed-end funds from the standpoint of likely reducing borrowing costs for levered funds, which increases the spread between their borrowing costs and then the rates that they can earn on the borrowed proceeds. If the Fed does go ahead and cut short-term rates, it should increase many funds earnings rates, which I believe will be viewed favorably by investors and help to create a solid backdrop for many fixed income closed-end funds.

In addition to the Fed potentially reducing short-term rates, another potential positive for the closed-end fund structure for the back half of 2025 relates to the increase in demand and improved overall sentiment. We could see if interest rates decline and yields on competing income-oriented investments such as certificates of deposits, money market funds, US treasuries, et cetera, were to decline. If we see these competing and alternative rates decline later this year, investors may be more likely to consider the closed-end fund structure given the very high and attractive distribution rates that exist in the secondary market for closed-end funds.

CEFA:

Jeff, in the current environment, what sectors do you think provide the best opportunities for income-oriented investors? And likewise for long-term investors?

Jeff Margolin:

I think there are many opportunities right now for closed-end fund investors. I am a big believer in building diversified closed-end fund portfolios by blending funds from different categories that oftentimes have different risk and reward characteristics. Right now, I particularly favor blending municipal closed-end funds, preferred closed-end funds, high yield closed-end funds, and equity income closed-end funds. I am particularly attracted right now to municipal closed-end funds, as I believe the bullish case for investing in municipal closed-end funds is quite strong and centered around three really straightforward bullet points. One, we have attractive valuations from my perspective in municipal closed-end funds. As mentioned earlier, municipal closed-end funds are the only broad category of the closed-end fund marketplace with average discounts to NAV, which are wider than their long-term average. Again, average discounts to NAV as of June 30th of -6% are wider and more expensive than the ten-year average of -5.3% and even wider than the twenty-year average of -3.6%.

So, I believe discounts for municipal closed-end funds could potentially narrow should the Federal Reserve lower short-term rates later this year. Two, the potential for short-term interest rate cuts. If the Federal Reserve reduces short-term interest rates this year, it would likely lead to lower leverage costs and increased earnings rates for leveraged municipal closed-end funds. I believe that would be viewed favorably by investors and increase demand for municipal closed-end funds in the secondary market.

And then finally, when I look at municipal closed-end funds, very compelling distribution rates. As of June 30th, the average municipal closed-end fund had a distribution rate of 6.3%, and I think it's important when we talk about that average distribution rate of 6.3%, to note that many municipal closed-end funds have an overall credit rating of investment grade, which allows investors to potentially earn an attractive distribution rate without having to take significant credit risk, in my opinion. So, in short, while municipal closed-end funds are my favorite category of the closed-end fund marketplace for the next 12 months, I just want to reiterate that I remain a long-term firm advocate for closed-end fund investors, building broadly diversified portfolios across many compelling categories, including but not limited to again, equity income, preferred, high yield, and municipal closed-end funds.

CEFA:

Jeff, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Jeff Margolin:

Thank you for having me. It was my pleasure.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning in to another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Alphabet Stock Looks Cheap Through A Long-Term Lens
data.description:
Introduction

Per my May article, Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) is navigating well in the AI era. The Q2 '25 numbers have come out since then, and progress is being made across numerous segments. My thesis is that Google looks undervalued when viewed through a long-term lens.

The Numbers

*ChatGPT reached 100 million users in January 2023 and it is a long-term threat to Google’s search business. However, Google’s “Search & other” revenue above in blue has increased nicely to this point, growing from $42.6 billion in 4Q22 to $54.2 billion in 2Q25.

Search & Other

Search & other revenue in 2Q25 was $54.2 billion which was 56% of the company’s overall revenue of $96.4 billion per the 2Q25 earnings release.

ChatGPT and others take market share away from Google search but it is crucial to differentiate between commercial and non-commercial market share. Up to this point, ChatGPT has hurt Google more with respect to market share of non-commercial interactions than with their market share of commercial interactions. At this time it seems to be more a matter of slowing Google’s Search & other revenue from what may have been high double digit Y/Y growth to what is now low double digit Y/Y growth.

A June note from @ChrisMPWC of Marnoa says Google keeps thriving despite threats from ChatGPT and others (emphasis added):

Despite these real challenges, Google’s search business continues to deliver results that exceed expectations. The reason? Current evidence indicates that Google’s AI tools such as AI Overviews and Gemini AI are proving additive - enhancing user engagement and expanding the scope of its core search business - rather than cannibalizing it.

The above Marnoa note goes on to say Google is now monetizing AI Overviews (“AIOs”) at about the same rate as traditional search results. It says Google still holds its dominant position with commercial/transactional queries. Additionally, the numbers show Generation Z embracing Google for commercial queries.

A June Search Engine Land article reveals how Google is getting creative in terms of mixing in the AIOs with the search engine result pages (“SERPs”). I recall the AIO always being at the top in the past, but now they are experimenting with it in different positions such as this example, where it is well below the fold:

A July WSJ article says the revenue threat from ChatGPT and others is easy to overstate (emphasis added):

The suggestion that AI tools are going to undermine Alphabet’s search business is easy to overstate. While traffic to OpenAI’s ChatGPT has grown massively, Google’s Gemini AI tools, and the AI overviews that appear in its searches, are gaining traction too. Gemini was drawing 350 million monthly users as of March, the company disclosed in court documents, versus an estimated 600 million monthly for ChatGPT. Some 1.5 billion people are using Google’s AI overviews monthly, Chief Executive Sundar Pichai said in April.

Speed matters. Allowing questions to be asked through the camera matters. Appealing to young users matters. CEO Sundar Pichai made encouraging points about these considerations in the Q2 '25 call (emphasis added):

AI Overviews are now powered by Gemini 2.5, delivering the fastest AI responses in the industry. We also saw strong growth in the use of multimodal Search, particularly the combination of Lens or Circle to Search, together with AI Overviews. This growth was most pronounced among younger users.

I expect Google’s Search & other revenue to continue climbing in the quarters ahead despite intense competition from ChatGPT and others.

YouTube

YouTube had 2Q25 ad revenue of $9.8 billion which was about 10% of Google’s overall revenue.

YouTube also has subscription revenue which isn’t given explicitly but I think it is about $4 to $5 billion each quarter. This is a subset of the “Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices” segment which had 2Q25 revenue of $11.2 billion. YouTube TV, YouTube Music, and YouTube Premium are all part of YouTube’s subscription revenue. One reason I think the cumulative figure is $4 to $5 billion is because of comments from management. Per the 3Q24 call, YouTube had trailing twelve month (“TTM”) revenue of more than $50 billion from the combination of ads and subscriptions. The TTM ad revenue for YouTube was $34.9 billion at that time which means the TTM subscription revenue was more than $15.1 billion.

Another data point comes from a July WSJ YouTube article from @benfritz which cites a MoffettNathanson revenue estimate of $54.2 billion for 2024. Ad revenue in 2024 was $36.1 meaning subscription revenue would have been about $18.1 billion. Seasonally adjusted YouTube ad revenue has been growing since these figures came out and it is highly likely their seasonally adjusted subscription revenue has been growing as well.

Including both ads and subscriptions, YouTube’s 2Q25 revenue was likely in the range of $14 to $15 billion which was about 15% of overall revenue. For a valuation context, Netflix had 2Q25 revenue of $11.1 billion and the market cap of Netflix (NFLX) is now around $500 billion.

YouTube CEO Neal Mohan spoke at the MoffettNathanson Media, Internet & Communications conference in May where he explained their flywheel as follows:

  1. Entice new and existing creators with toolset improvements.
  2. Attract more viewers with new content and innovative formats like connected TVs. Shorts and Podcasts are priorities.
  3. Monetization.

Citing Nielsen data, @viggy_krishnan has a post showing what this flywheel has done from May 2021 to May 2025. In May 2021, YouTube had 6% of US TV viewing and streaming as a whole had 26%. By May 2025, four years later, YouTube more than doubled to 12.5% while streaming as a whole increased to 44.8%.

A June blog post from YouTube CEO Neal Mohan @nealmohan talks about how YouTube is growing with AI. They have an auto dubbing feature which translates videos across 9 different languages with another 11 languages coming soon. Veo 3 is an especially exciting development:

Veo is Google Deepmind’s video generation model which lets you create AI-generated backgrounds and video clips for Shorts. We’ve put this model into the hands of creators with Dream Screen. Last month, Google announced the newest model: Veo 3, which vastly improves video quality and incorporates audio. Today, I’m proud to share that Veo 3 will be coming to YouTube Shorts later this summer.

The July WSJ YouTube article mentioned above shows the dominant position YouTube has reached with respect to U.S. TV viewing:

There are two more salient data points from the July WSJ YouTube article above. YouTube shares about 55% of commercial revenue with creators which means revenue incentives are aligned. YouTube pays the NFL about $2 billion each year to be the exclusive home of the NFL Sunday Ticket.

The 55% rev share arrangement often encourages creators to make longer videos which offer more placements for effective “mid-roll” ads.

The NFL deal is something which only works well for providers with a large number of viewers such as YouTube and Netflix. Per CEO Sundar Pichai’s comments in the Q2 '25 call, YouTube is streaming the NFL's first Friday game of the season, live from Brazil. I expect to see both YouTube and Netflix use their size advantage in the years ahead with content related to sports.

During the Q2 '25 call, CEO Pichai talked about the Veo 3 considerations mentioned earlier along with Gemini’s ability to turn photos into videos:

I also want to mention Veo 3, our state-of-the-art video generation model. It's been a viral hit, with people sharing clips created in the Gemini app, and with our new AI filmmaking tool, Flow. Since May, over 70 million videos have been generated using Veo 3. And we recently introduced a feature in the Gemini app to turn photos into videos, which people absolutely love. It's also rolling out to Google Photos users starting today.

Given Netflix’s market cap of about half a trillion, I think YouTube could be worth between half a trillion and three-fourths of a trillion. And YouTube’s ad and subscription revenue only accounts for about 15% of Google’s revenue!

Google Cloud

Google’s Q2 '25 cloud revenue was $13.6 billion, which was about 14% of total revenue. Cloud operating income went up from $2.2 billion in Q1 '25 to $2.8 billion in Q2 '25. On an annualized basis, Google Cloud had operating income of $11.3 billion on revenue of $54.5 billion.

The operating margin increases at Google Cloud have been wonderful and one of the reasons they keep going up is because some costs stay close to fixed while revenue increases. The operating margin increased from 5% in 2Q23 to 11% in 2Q24 to 21% in 2Q25!

During the Q2 '25 call, CEO Pichai talked about OpenAI using Google Cloud for some of their needs:

With respect to OpenAI, look, we are very excited to be partnering with them on Google Cloud. Google Cloud is an open platform, and we have a strong history of supporting great companies; start-ups, AI labs, et cetera. So, super excited about our partnership there on the cloud side. We look forward to investing more in that relationship and growing there.

Unlike other cloud providers, Google doesn’t rely exclusively on Nvidia’s (NVDA) GPUs on the AI side. Google has been designing their own TPUs for many years but they have mostly been used internally apart from some big announcements from companies like Apple (AAPL). During the 2Q25 call, CEO Pichai said AI research labs like Safe Superintelligence and Physical Intelligence use Google’s TPUs. Safe Superintelligence is very well known and Founder Ilya Sutskever has a great deal of influence.

I’m optimistic for the future economics of all three hyperscalers. In other words, I expect Google Cloud to continue doing well along with Amazon AWS (AMZN) and Microsoft Azure (MSFT).

Other Bets Including Waymo

Waymo’s fleet is tiny compared to Uber’s (UBER), but they are already being disruptive in key markets like San Francisco. Investor Alex Immerman @aleximm from a16z made a December post showing they way Waymo has passed Lyft (LYFT) in San Francisco as they continue to gain on Uber:

Rather than having a huge fleet like Uber (UBER), I think Waymo will have a smaller number of vehicles but each of their vehicles should be more heavily utilized than the typical Uber vehicle.

Waymo and Toyota (TM) made a strategic partnership announcement in April. One obvious takeaway is Toyota helping Waymo get ready for the launch of their robotaxi service in Tokyo. Additionally, the announcement also mentions the licensing of Waymo’s tech for personally owned vehicles (emphasis added):

In parallel, the companies will explore how to leverage Waymo's autonomous technology and Toyota's vehicle expertise to enhance next-generation personally owned vehicles (“POVs”).

A June WSJ article talks about how Waymo’s valuation has increased since late October when they announced a $5.6 billion funding round which valued them at about $45 billion. Waymo is majority-owned by Alphabet and they have over 1,500 vehicles in their fleet with plans to double it by the end of 2026. A base case valuation of $150 billion from Raymond James is mentioned in the article (emphasis added):

Some Wall Street analysts covering Alphabet have higher estimates now, given Waymo’s strong lead in the robotaxi service market and a partnership with Uber that is expected to boost usage. Josh Beck of Raymond James put a “base case” valuation of Waymo around $150 billion in a report last month. He projects the company’s gross bookings will average 129% growth annually for the next five years.

The WSJ article above has Waymo revenue figures and estimates from Morgan Stanley. 2024 revenue was about $100 million and estimates for 2025, 2026 and 2027 are $290 million, $500 million and $790 million, respectively.

Currently the cost of each new Waymo is well above the cost of each new Tesla. A June Bloomberg article shows the difference with respect to hardware between Tesla’s 8-camera suite and Waymo’s 5th generation suite:

A June article from Time talks about each new Waymo vehicle costing more than $120 thousand but I think this number is coming down drastically in the near future:

With their cutting-edge AI technologies and sensors, each Waymo car is eye-poppingly expensive: more than $120,000, analysts estimate.

I believe the cost of each new Waymo vehicle will continue to decline as they switch from expensive Jaguars to more reasonably priced Hyundais (OTCPK:HYMTF). Additionally, the sensors continue to get cheaper each year.

I am optimistic that new Waymo vehicles will cost less than $100 thousand each fairly soon. This may not happen while they add 1,500 vehicles to the fleet by the end of 2026. However, if they continue to add 1,500 vehicles to the fleet each year starting in 2027 and the cost is about $100 thousand per vehicle then this is a yearly figure of about $150 million which should become manageable. Of course another way to think about it is $100 million or so for every addition of 1,000 vehicles.

I don’t think this is a winner-take-all market and I believe Waymo can have plenty of success with a fleet which is much smaller than the current fleet at Uber. Tesla (TSLA) will always have a cost advantage, but I believe many riders will pay up for extra safety hardware.

During the Q2 '25 call, CEO Pichai said Waymo launched in Atlanta last year. Additionally, they have more than doubled their service zone in Austin while expanding in LA and the SF Bay Area by 50%. CEO Pichai said the Waymo Driver has now gone over 100 million miles and testing is being done in ten new cities including New York and Philadelphia.

We don’t need to continue losing 40,000 lives on U.S. roads each year. I believe companies like Waymo and Tesla will make our roads significantly safer in the long run.

Valuation

One obvious valuation risk is the ongoing threat from ChatGPT, but we’ve gone through some of the misunderstandings, and I think there is room for both ChatGPT and Google to thrive in the future. Another valuation risk is the return on capex investments. If Google’s accrual accounting is accurate then much of the expected $85 billion in 2025 capex is growth capex as opposed to maintenance capex. Given this consideration then the adjusted free cash flow (“FCF”) where we only subtract the maintenance capex is much higher than the raw FCF where we subtract all the capex. I’m optimistic that the accounting is largely correct and I expect to see solid returns from the growth capex investments. Yet another risk is the bidding war we’re seeing with AI researchers.

A July WSJ article says Windsurf made a deal with Google after talks with OpenAI stalled. A small number of Windsurf employees will work on agentic coding at Google’s DeepMind:

Google has agreed to pay about $2.4 billion in a deal to license the technology of AI coding startup Windsurf and hire its CEO and some of its employees, according to people familiar with the matter.

The bidding wars are concerning, but I think Google will strike the right balance in terms of employing many of the top AI researchers and giving them fair compensation which is in line with how much value these folks bring to the company.

These are some of the numbers I look at with respect to valuation:

Again, the raw FCF figure of $5.3 billion for 2Q25 is meager. In the 2Q25 call, management said they expect to have capex of $85 billion for 2025 which is up substantially from the $52.5 billion in 2024.

TTM operating income for the services segment is $129.4 billion on TTM revenue of $320.4 billion. However, I assign the TTM company-wide operating losses of $12.3 billion to this segment for an adjusted TTM segment operating income of $117.1 billion. Applying a multiple of 19 to 21x gives us a valuation range of $2,225 to $2,460 billion when rounded to the nearest $5 billion. Again, YouTube is a sizable part of this segment from a valuation perspective.

I think Google Cloud is worth 20 to 22x the annualized operating income of $11.3 billion, which gives us a range of $225 to $250 billion when rounded to the nearest $5 billion.

Waymo by itself may be worth $150 billion or more, but Alphabet does not have 100% ownership. Still, the Other Bets segment has other businesses such as quantum computing. I think the segment is worth $150 to $200 billion.

Summing up, I value Google as follows:

$2,225 to $2,460 billion services

$225 to $250 billion cloud

$150 to $200 billion other bets

-------------------------------------

$2,600 to $2,910 billion total

The Q2 '25 10-Q shows 5,817 million A shares, 847 million B shares and 5,430 million C shares through July 16. We multiply the 6,664 million A & B shares times the July 24 GOOGL share price of $192.17 to get a partial consideration of $1,281 billion. We multiply the 5,430 million C shares times the July 24 GOOG share price of $193.20 to get another partial consideration of $1,049 billion. Adding the two partial considerations together, we have a market cap of $2,330 billion. The market cap is less than my valuation range, and I think the stock is a Buy for long-term investors who plan to hold it for at least three years.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alphabet Q2: That's Why I Keep The Company In My Recommended Portfolio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my recommendation to Buy Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL, NASDAQ:GOOG) shares after the release of its Q2 2025 results. This article is a continuation of my introductory coverage article published on December 12, 2024, with the same recommendation.

The company released its results on July 23, 2025, and they exceeded market expectations, as we'll see throughout this article. Amidst the many challenges facing AI and user behavior, this article aims to present Alphabet's results, along with opinions and perspectives.

Net Revenue - Growth In All Segments

Net revenue rose 12% YoY to $96.43 billion, while estimates had pointed to $94.04 billion. Growth was seen across all businesses, including Search Advertising, which grew +12% YoY, driven by engagement with search features like AI Overviews, which already has 2 billion monthly users.

Other growth drivers include YouTube Ads (+13% YoY) and Google Play (+20 % YoY). However, the fastest-growing segment is still Google Cloud, which reached $13.6 billion (+32% YoY).

My assessment of this segment of the results is very positive. Gemini usage increased 35x YoY with the adoption of AI workflows for corporate clients, and as for outlook, CEO Pinchai stated that he expects further growth in the user base. CFO Ashkenazi indicated a possible positive impact from exchange rates in 3Q25, which is consistent with my thesis of dollar depreciation.

Operating Profit - Impressive Jump In Cloud

The company reported a 14% YoY increase in operating profit to $31.27 billion, equivalent to a margin of 32.4%. When analyzing business lines, we see a contribution from Google Services, but a significant contribution from Google Cloud given its size compared to other segments.

Google Cloud's results are so impressive that its operating margin jumped from 11.3% in Q2 24 to 20.7% in Q2 25. While Google Services maintained its operating margin at 40%, which I consider quite adequate.

My outlook for this segment is also positive, based on comments from company executives. Management indicated in the Q&A that the focus is on balancing AI talent acquisition with efficiency.

CapEx - The Point Of Attention

The main point of note in the results is the updated investment outlook. The company now estimates investing $85 billion in 2025 (previously $75 billion). CEO Pinchai stated that the focus will be on expanding into AI, and that investments in 2026 are expected to be even higher.

As an analyst, I must say that the numbers are quite frightening. However, I follow the line of my articles about the company, in which I praise the quality of its management. In fact, Warren Buffett frequently talks about the importance of management in his investments, and I believe that Alphabet's management deserves the benefit of the doubt in this case.

Net Income

Finally, the company reported a 19% YoY increase in net income to $28.2 billion. This represents earnings per share of $2.31, above market expectations of $2.20.

My assessment of the company's results is very positive. The company exceeded analysts' expectations, reporting double-digit growth in net revenue and net income. Management's plans seem both promising and diligent. The figure below provides a general summary of the results, and then we move on to the valuation.

Valuation

For the valuation, I used a DCF model with a 5-year projection and terminal value based on perpetual growth. Projections include 12% revenue growth in 2025, gradually decelerating through 2029.

Additionally, the EBITDA margin expands from 37% to 49.9%, reflecting the operational efficiency gains that will come from AI. Speaking of AI, CapEx is projected to include $85 billion in 2025 and $90 billion in 2026, as indicated by management. More assumptions are below.

The model indicates a fair value of $223.31 per share, a 17.4% upside compared to the current price. Therefore, I reiterate my Buy recommendation for Alphabet shares based on the strong results reported and the attractive valuation, according to my assumptions.

I emphasize that the recommendation is consistent with my recommended stock portfolio for 2025, with Alphabet among its holdings. Year to date, the portfolio is up 13.8%, while the SPY ETF is up 8.36%.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Without a doubt, the main risk to this thesis is the failure to realize returns on investments, especially if the AI and Cloud segments don't grow at the expected pace. It's worth noting that supply chain challenges pose an additional adversity at this point.

Still on the topic of AI, I see monetizing AI products as another challenge for the company. If the user experience isn't well-designed, intense competition could lead to a decline in user engagement. Finally, I believe that retaining AI talent will be vital to the company's success.

Among other issues, legal settlement expenses are another risk to monitor, especially with antitrust and regulatory issues. The thesis is quite complex, and investors should analyze it carefully.

The Bottom Line

Alphabet reported strong results, exceeding analyst expectations and posting double-digit growth in net revenue and net income. Google Cloud was a standout, with its operating margin nearly doubling year over year.

The announcement of an increase in CapEx from $75 billion to $85 billion by 2025 did not scare investors this time. Incorporating this data and other assumptions into the DCF model, we have a 17% upside, which seems attractive to me for this thesis.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my Buy recommendation for Alphabet shares. As with other articles, I must emphasize the importance of investors trusting the company's management and its investment approach.

data.title: Netflix: More Challenges As Streaming Competition Heats Up
data.description:
Netflix's (NASDAQ:NFLX) Q2 results may have energized some, despite a few small negatives:

Revenue of $11.08 billion, and beating the forecast on earnings with EPS of $7.19.

But the US streaming market is saturated, with 96% of 124 million total available households. There's hardly any exponential growth left with global room for growth only. Netflix hard charge ahead to focus on global markets and pricing power moves in the US and Canada. But now as peers are playing better catch-up ball, the gaps in total customers will begin to narrow between the top 10 streamers.

We have now entered a final phase of growth where the average household already has at least four services. So, exponential growth in a great part of the US will slow down. And Netflix, whose focus on volume and margin, will need to change course to maintain its runaway leadership of the sector. But can it? I think not. Not because it fails with managing the task. It by far is one of the best managed companies in the field on every measure.

Netflix will depart the exponential trading range it now holds to decline significantly while saturation globally continues and peers gain share of market each month as they introduce new content.

This means Netflix shareholders now need to adjust its expected trading range for the future price disparity of the stock, which will narrow considerably. It will descend into a regular price range close to its peers. We have seen this "price democracy" form in many sectors over time. So, expectations for investors need to be curbed.

If you've been in Netflix since the early 2000s, and now breaking a big smile at well over $1,200, start to think about a watchful hold. If you are in lower, think about selling off a chunk and putting it into the "cash is king" account. If you're really up since the stock traded under $300, run for the hills and figure a buyback strategy if you smell weakness as you review the free cash flow underperformance.

The point is this: Do you believe Netflix is vastly overbought now or do you believe there are gallons upon gallons of gas left in the tank?

Netflix overbought, overpriced: Time for a good hard look

The truth: Netflix is a brilliantly conceived business model difficult to pick flaws in if you're building a bear case because you're a happy holder. Thus far, current competitors have proven themselves incapable of coming close. Peers are catching up as the universe of total streaming customers expands globally. Yet the US market has little unexploited territory to offer challengers. So what we will see is Netflix and its peers trading like utilities together in a sector perhaps three years from now. Growth in subs will be a tough ticket for newbies. Part of the reason: The 96% saturation. Expect this to begin waning next year.

Of course, we recognize the different lanes in which Netflix runs and the pure play advantage it may have in the heads of many investors who don't have to consider the main business performance of the parent. Simplicity is the heart of the Netflix model. It was the same when it challenged Blockbuster with mail exchange of red envelopes by mail.

It was the most logical switch of highways for Netflix from the built-in US postal service to the TV screen. It was an incredible decision to use what was there rather than reinvent the wheel by creating a network. Yet a part of a simple system of clicking a handful of buttons on your TV was to open a vast world of entertainment at your fingertips suited to fit snugly in your lifestyle.

Then build a price list within the affordability of everyone and slowly raise prices to compensate for rising production costs. And heavily promote features and exclusive films. But most critically, think of your service as the marketing of an Amazon by streaming the process free of bumps, obstacles, and odd thinking and giving you time to mull your choices, which are at least 14.

The trends are beginning: Runaway prices on Netflix shock trading in prices way beyond peers due to the early mover experience, transforming to sustained market share dominance from peers. Phase two: Runaway shares begin to reach excessive heights and share of market begins to fall. At the same time, peers begin trending higher, bringing a balanced price for all.

The era of towering disparity in pricing evens out. We move into a utility stock phase within a few years. Investors now need to shave down their hope for appreciation ahead to something more realistic. with history of past unicorns.

Netflix: $1,180

Disney (DIS): $122 (conglomerate but trades up on streaming growth)

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD): $12.85

Paramount (PARA) :$13.26

Comcast (CMCSA): $35.754

Above: The streaming sector is diverse between pure play Netflix and key peers with diverse entertainment giants with footprints in movies, TV productions, live entertainment, TV network shows, etc. While the analogy isn't perfect between a pure-play stock and one that's part of a conglomerate, that's how they compete. They all present the exact same product, similar promotions, and state taxes with few exceptions. Prepare yourself with lowered expectations than when you opened your first position.

Conclusion

We think the big runs for Netflix are over and that the next growth phase will spread across the sector entirely as subscriber growth widens for peers. Netflix has to find it harder to poach shares from awakened peers.

Our view: Time to take a dollar-for-dollar audit of your gains and decide whether to sit tight or begin to unload chunks of stock safely in the bag.
